metadata.title: WattWise 2026 NEC Research-Backed Content Foundation
metadata.validationStatus: Verified against publicly accessible official NFPA, licensing-board, exam-vendor, NCCER, and Apprenticeship.gov sources available on 2026-03-30. Final article-by-article validation against the full paid 2026 NEC text is still required before production claims of complete coverage.
limitations: This pack intentionally avoids invented 50-state exam claims where no official source was verified during this turn.
The practice bank is a vetted starter bank, not the final 500-1000+ item production bank requested.
Jurisdiction requirements remain dynamic and should be re-checked before release in each target state.

executiveSummary.overview: WattWise should use a national-core curriculum anchored to the 2026 NEC, with jurisdiction overlays for exam approval rules, question counts, open-book policies, required references, and renewal or continuing-education differences. Publicly accessible official NFPA development documents for the 2026 NEC show a major usability and content shift around over-1000-volt and 1500 Vdc systems, EV power transfer, microgrids and hybrid power terminology, and Article 680 errata, all of which affect exam navigation and code-lookup training.
examPrepImpact: Candidates need stronger code-navigation training because 2026 development materials show major renumbering and reorganization in medium-voltage and overcurrent/grounding content.
Journeyman coverage must be first-class in the product. Official Texas and Maine sources confirm journeyman exams remain central and materially different from apprentice or master expectations.
A national simulator should be labeled as a national-core exam, while jurisdiction overlays control question counts, timing, pass thresholds, and approved references.
instructionalUseNotes: Use lessons to teach code path and reasoning first, then use practice to reinforce section lookup speed.
Keep every lesson tied to NEC article references, but summarize rather than quote copyrighted code text.
Mark jurisdictional data with the exact source date and remind users that local amendments may apply.

nec2026Changes.title: High-voltage reorganization around over-1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc systems
nec2026Changes.summary: Official NFPA development materials for the 2026 NEC show a coordinated usability reorganization for medium and high-voltage requirements, using over 1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc as a recurring threshold and moving related material into dedicated articles instead of scattered parts.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Master and advanced journeyman candidates should expect more article-navigation work involving reorganized high-voltage, grounding, and protective-device rules.

nec2026Changes.title: New Article 270 for grounding and bonding of systems over 1000 Vac, 1500 Vdc nominal
nec2026Changes.summary: NFPA working-draft material for the 2026 cycle shows Article 270 created as a dedicated location for grounding and bonding requirements for systems over 1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc nominal.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Users will need updated map skills for grounding and bonding questions that were previously distributed differently across the code.

nec2026Changes.title: Overcurrent and overvoltage content moved into Article 245
nec2026Changes.summary: NFPA correlating-committee public-input documentation describes relocating over-1000-volt overcurrent and overvoltage material into a new Article 245 and renumbering related equipment content.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Advanced calculations and protection-coordination drills should use updated article paths so candidates do not waste time searching outdated locations.

nec2026Changes.title: EV and bidirectional power-transfer vocabulary expanded
nec2026Changes.summary: NFPA 2026 public-input material for CMP-12 expands EVSE context and references bidirectional EVSE or wireless power transfer equipment, reflecting broader charging and power-export use cases.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: WattWise should add EVSE, power-export, and terminology flashcards and keep Article 625 navigation current.

nec2026Changes.title: New Article 627 proposal for electric self-propelled vehicle power transfer systems
nec2026Changes.summary: NFPA development records for the 2026 cycle include a new Article 627 proposal to cover battery-powered self-propelled vehicles beyond on-road electric vehicles, such as forklifts and similar equipment.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Special-equipment and emerging-technology practice content should separate Article 625 roadway EV scenarios from broader vehicle power-transfer systems when final text is confirmed.

nec2026Changes.title: Microgrid and hybrid-power definitions refined
nec2026Changes.summary: Official NFPA public-input records show notable definitional work around microgrids, hybrid power systems, and related control and interconnect devices.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Master-level design lessons should include updated terminology and question stems that distinguish traditional standby systems from microgrid and hybrid architectures.

nec2026Changes.title: 2026 errata already affects Article 680 pool content
nec2026Changes.summary: NFPA has already issued 2026 errata affecting 680.24 and 680.26 references and wording, confirming that training content must track errata and not rely only on first-print text.
nec2026Changes.examImpact: Pool and spa bonding content must note that errata can change citation details and should be reviewed during code updates.

curriculumFramework.courseDescription: Foundational electrical theory, safety, tools, code literacy, and beginner calculations aligned to entry-level trade preparation.

learningObjectives: Explain voltage, current, resistance, power, and energy using trade language.
Apply Ohm's Law and Watt's Law to simple series and parallel circuits.
Recognize the difference between theory concepts and NEC installation rules.

lessons.lessonTitle: Voltage, Current, Resistance, and Ohm's Law
Power, Energy, and Basic Circuit Relationships

learningObjectives: Identify electrical shock, arc-flash, and arc-blast hazards.
Connect OSHA work-practice expectations with jobsite behavior.
Explain why de-energization and verification matter before beginning work.

lessons.lessonTitle: Electrical Hazard Recognition and Safe Work Basics
Shock Paths, PPE, and Lockout Mindset

learningObjectives: Navigate articles, parts, sections, tables, and informational notes.
Differentiate enforceable rules from explanatory material.
Find foundational definitions and general installation rules quickly.

lessons.lessonTitle: How the NEC Is Organized
Code Lookup Drill: Article 100 and Article 110

learningObjectives: Recognize common raceways, cable assemblies, and conductor types.
Use Chapter 3 articles to identify permitted uses and support rules.
Connect conductor sizing to insulation, ampacity, and environment.

lessons.lessonTitle: Raceways, Cables, and Boxes
Conductor Sizing and Table Navigation

curriculumFramework.courseDescription: Applied NEC navigation, installation requirements, calculations, motors, transformers, and troubleshooting for working electricians preparing for licensure.

learningObjectives: Move between Chapters 1 through 4 and special rules in Chapters 5 through 7 efficiently.

Spot when a general rule is modified by a special occupancy or equipment article.

lessons.lessonTitle: General Rules vs Special Rules
Timed Code Lookup Strategies

learningObjectives: Apply branch-circuit, feeder, and continuous-load sizing rules.
Use Article 210, 215, and 220 together during load questions.
Evaluate receptacle, small-appliance, and dwelling-unit circuit requirements.

lessons.lessonTitle: Continuous Loads and Overcurrent Device Sizing
Dwelling Receptacles and Small-Appliance Circuits

learningObjectives: Separate grounding functions from bonding functions.

lessons.lessonTitle: Grounding vs Bonding at Services and Feeders
Grounding Electrodes and Electrode Conductors

learningObjectives: Locate full-load current, conductor sizing, overload, and short-circuit rules.
Use motor tables instead of nameplate current where required by the NEC.
Explain the difference between overload protection and short-circuit protection.

lessons.lessonTitle: Motor Circuit Conductors and Overload Protection
Motor Branch-Circuit Short-Circuit and Ground-Fault Protection

curriculumFramework.courseDescription: Advanced code interpretation, system design, complex calculations, special occupancies, overcurrent coordination, and business-facing responsibilities.

learningObjectives: Evaluate service, feeder, and distribution concepts at a system level.

Integrate emerging 2026 organization changes into code-navigation habits.

lessons.lessonTitle: Service and Feeder Design Workflow
Using Tables, Notes, and Exceptions Efficiently

learningObjectives: Perform multifamily, commercial, and mixed-use load calculations.

lessons.lessonTitle: Dwelling Optional Calculation Workflow
Commercial Demand and Feeder Sizing

learningObjectives: Identify Class, Division, and Zone concepts at a code-navigation level.

lessons.lessonTitle: Hazardous Location Classification Fundamentals
Motors, Seals, and Equipment Selection in Classified Areas

learningObjectives: Use scope, purpose, and hierarchy rules to resolve code questions.
Recognize when jurisdictional policy or adopted amendments change the answer path.
Tie technical compliance decisions to plan review, inspection, and business risk.

lessons.lessonTitle: Reading 90.1, 90.3, and Article 100 Like a Master
When the NEC Is Not the Whole Answer

fullLessonContent.lessonTitle: Code Lookup Drill: Article 100 and Article 110
learningObjectives: Locate definitions before interpreting an installation rule.
Use Article 110 as the starting point for many general-requirement questions.
Explain why exam candidates should not answer from memory when the code book is available.

lessonContent.heading: Why this lesson matters
lessonContent.body: Many apprentice candidates lose points because they search randomly. The faster path is often Article 100 for definitions, then the relevant installation article, then Article 110 when the question asks for general requirements such as working space, equipment installation, or listing and labeling.

lessonContent.heading: Step 1: Find the defined term
lessonContent.body: If a question uses words like outlet, receptacle, disconnecting means, readily accessible, or continuous load, start in Article 100. On open-book exams, accurate definitions prevent you from chasing the wrong article.

lessonContent.heading: Step 2: Move to the rule article
lessonContent.body: After confirming the term, move to the article that governs the installation. For a branch-circuit receptacle question, that may be Article 210. For conductor sizing, you may move to Article 310. For boxes, Article 314 is often involved. Article 110 frequently supports or modifies the answer with general installation requirements.

lessonContent.heading: Real-world application
lessonContent.body: An apprentice asked to install new equipment should verify listing instructions, space, access, and workmanship before pulling conductors. The exam mirrors that workflow by expecting you to combine specific articles with Article 110.

keyTakeaways: Definitions first, installation rule second, general rules third.
Article 110 is one of the fastest score boosters on code-lookup exams.
Open-book speed comes from article pattern recognition, not from guessing.
practiceQuestions: Which article should you check first if a question depends on the meaning of readily accessible?
What general-requirement article often pairs with installation-specific articles on exams?

fullLessonContent.lessonTitle: Electrical Hazard Recognition and Safe Work Basics
learningObjectives: Identify common shock and arc hazards before beginning work.
Explain the difference between de-energized work and presumed-safe work.
Connect code compliance with safer installations and safer maintenance conditions.

lessonContent.heading: Hazard recognition comes before tools
lessonContent.body: The first safety decision is not what tool to grab but whether the circuit is energized, whether the enclosure is suitable, and whether enough working space exists to inspect, test, or maintain equipment without creating a new hazard.

lessonContent.heading: Listing and labeling are safety topics
lessonContent.body: Candidates often treat listing and labeling as paperwork. In reality, equipment used outside its listing or outside manufacturer instructions can create shock, overheating, and failure hazards. That is why the NEC repeatedly directs installers back to listed equipment and installation instructions.

lessonContent.heading: Exam habit
lessonContent.body: When a question asks what is safest or most compliant, look for the answer that removes exposure first, then the one that follows equipment instructions, and then the one that preserves access and working space.

keyTakeaways: Safe work starts with condition assessment, not speed.
Manufacturer instructions are enforceable when the code says listed equipment must be installed that way.
Working-space questions are both code and safety questions.
practiceQuestions: Why can a listing-and-labeling question also be a safety question?
What article should you check when clearance and access are involved?

fullLessonContent.lessonTitle: Continuous Loads and Overcurrent Device Sizing
learningObjectives: Define continuous load correctly.
Apply the 125 percent sizing concept to branch-circuit and feeder overcurrent devices where required.
Avoid mixing conductor ampacity rules with overcurrent sizing rules.

lessonContent.heading: Definition first
lessonContent.body: A continuous load is one where the maximum current is expected to continue for 3 hours or more. This definition drives several sizing decisions and appears often in both residential and commercial exam problems.

lessonContent.heading: Branch-circuit overcurrent sizing
lessonContent.body: For branch circuits, the rating of the overcurrent device must be sufficient for the noncontinuous load plus 125 percent of the continuous load when the applicable rule requires it. Candidates should read the question closely and determine whether it is asking about the overcurrent device, the conductor, or both.

lessonContent.heading: Feeder follow-through
lessonContent.body: The same logic continues into feeder problems. Journeyman candidates should practice moving from Article 220 load calculations to Article 215 sizing rules without losing track of whether the problem is asking for minimum ampacity, minimum overcurrent device rating, or both.

lessonContent.heading: Worked example
lessonContent.body: If the calculated continuous load is 48 amperes and there is no noncontinuous load, 48 x 1.25 = 60 amperes. The candidate then checks the applicable article for the conductor and overcurrent device path instead of stopping at the arithmetic.

keyTakeaways: Always identify whether a load is continuous before sizing.
125 percent shows up in multiple places, but you still need the correct article.
Calculation questions are often won or lost in the code path after the math.
practiceQuestions: A continuous load of 32 A is supplied by a branch circuit. What minimum overcurrent device rating should you consider first under the continuous-load rule?
Which article should you pair with Article 220 when the problem becomes a feeder-sizing question?

fullLessonContent.lessonTitle: Grounding vs Bonding at Services and Feeders
learningObjectives: Differentiate grounding from bonding using NEC terminology.
Identify the service bonding jumper and grounding electrode conductor roles.
Recognize why separately derived systems and feeders create common exam traps.

lessonContent.heading: The distinction the exam tests repeatedly
lessonContent.body: Grounding ties an electrical system to earth for stabilization and voltage limitation. Bonding joins conductive parts together to create an effective fault-current path. Candidates who use the words as if they mean the same thing routinely miss service and feeder questions.

lessonContent.heading: Service equipment focus
lessonContent.body: At service equipment, the grounded conductor, service disconnecting means, and grounding electrode system relationship must be understood as a system, not as isolated parts. Read the question closely to see whether it is asking about the neutral-to-case bonding location, the electrode path, or equipment grounding conductors on the load side.

lessonContent.heading: Feeder and building questions
lessonContent.body: When a feeder supplies another building or structure, candidates must slow down and follow the specific building or structure feeder rule instead of assuming the service rule applies unchanged. That is why Article 250 question stems often test whether you know when to leave the service article and move deeper into the article.

keyTakeaways: Grounding and bonding are related but not interchangeable terms.
Service questions often require more than one section of Article 250.
Feeder-to-building questions are common traps because they look like service questions.
practiceQuestions: What is the exam-safe definition difference between grounding and bonding?
Which section should you check for feeders to separate buildings or structures?

learningObjectives: Use a repeatable workflow for optional dwelling calculations.
Separate general load, small-appliance, laundry, appliances, dryers, ranges, and HVAC adjustments.
Translate the final load into service or feeder ampacity.

lessonContent.heading: Why workflow matters
lessonContent.body: Master exam problems are rarely failed because of simple multiplication. They are failed because the candidate uses the wrong calculation method, misses a category, or applies a demand factor to the wrong subtotal.

lessonContent.heading: Recommended sequence
lessonContent.body: Start with the applicable optional-method rule, identify whether the occupancy qualifies, total the general and required loads, process appliances and fixed equipment correctly, then compare heating and cooling under the applicable method. Only after the load is complete should you move to conductor and overcurrent device sizing.

lessonContent.heading: Exam discipline
lessonContent.body: Master-level candidates should write the categories in order before solving. That keeps you from skipping a laundry circuit, double-counting a fastened-in-place appliance, or confusing the calculation article with the conductor article.

keyTakeaways: Pick the right method before doing math.
Keep calculation articles separate from conductor-sizing articles until the load is complete.
Write the category list before solving.
practiceQuestions: What common error occurs when a candidate moves to conductor sizing before the optional calculation is complete?
Which article governs the optional calculation itself?

learningObjectives: Distinguish class, division, and zone concepts at a test-taking level.
Use Article 500 as the common starting point before moving to occupancy-specific articles.
Recognize when the question is about classification and when it is about equipment selection.

lessonContent.heading: Classification before equipment
lessonContent.body: If you do not know whether the location is Class I, II, or III, and Division 1 or 2, equipment-selection rules will not help you. Start with the classification concept, then move to the article and section that addresses permitted equipment or sealing requirements.

lessonContent.heading: How the code path usually works
lessonContent.body: Article 500 gives the general framework and references the occupancy-specific articles. From there, the question may drive you to 501, 502, 503, 505, or 506 depending on the hazard and the classification method used by the jurisdiction or problem statement.

lessonContent.heading: Real-world application
lessonContent.body: A designer or master electrician working with a classified location does not guess. They confirm the process hazard, verify the classified area, and then choose wiring methods, seals, fittings, motors, and luminaires appropriate to that classification.

keyTakeaways: Article 500 is the map; the later articles are the destination.
Hazardous-location questions often hide the real task in the wording.
practiceQuestions: What should you determine before selecting equipment for a classified location?
Which article should usually be your starting map for classified-location questions?

questionBank.questionText: A question depends on the NEC meaning of the term 'readily accessible.' Which location should you check first?
questionBank.explanation: Defined terms should be checked in Article 100 before you interpret the installation rule. That prevents using an everyday meaning when the NEC has a technical one.

questionBank.questionText: Which NEC section is commonly used for questions about required working space around electrical equipment?
questionBank.explanation: Section 110.26 addresses access and working space around electrical equipment. It is one of the most common general-requirement lookup points on electrician exams.

questionBank.questionText: In dwelling-unit habitable rooms, receptacle placement rules are found primarily in which section?
questionBank.explanation: Section 210.52(A) addresses dwelling-unit receptacle spacing requirements for wall spaces in habitable rooms.

questionBank.questionText: Box-fill calculations for conductor volume are found in which NEC section?
questionBank.explanation: Section 314.16 covers box volume and conductor-fill requirements. This is a core apprentice code-lookup topic.

questionBank.questionText: Which NEC table is commonly used to look up general conductor ampacity values before applying adjustment or correction factors?
questionBank.explanation: Table 310.16 is the common starting point for general conductor ampacity lookup. The user must still read the surrounding sections and applicable conditions.

questionBank.questionText: Under the NEC, a continuous load is one where the maximum current is expected to continue for how long?
questionBank.explanation: Article 100 defines a continuous load as one where the maximum current is expected to continue for 3 hours or more.

questionBank.questionText: A branch circuit supplies a 40-ampere continuous load and no noncontinuous load. Ignoring conductor-terminal limitations and using the NEC continuous-load rule, what is the minimum overcurrent device rating to consider first?
questionBank.explanation: For a continuous load, start with 125 percent: 40 x 1.25 = 50 A. The code path then continues through the applicable overcurrent and conductor rules.

questionBank.questionText: Which NEC section should you check when a feeder supplies a separate building or structure?
questionBank.explanation: Section 250.32 addresses buildings or structures supplied by feeder or branch circuit. Candidates often miss this by staying in the service-equipment section.

questionBank.questionText: When sizing motor branch-circuit conductors, electricians are often directed to use what value rather than only the nameplate current?

answerChoices.B: Full-load current from the applicable NEC table

questionBank.explanation: Motor conductor and protection rules often rely on the applicable NEC full-load current tables rather than only the nameplate current. Journeyman candidates should know where to find those motor tables and rules.

questionBank.questionText: Which article is the main starting point for feeder and service load calculations?
questionBank.explanation: Article 220 contains the load-calculation rules used before moving to conductor and overcurrent sizing articles.

questionBank.questionText: Before starting a dwelling optional calculation problem, what should a master candidate determine first?

answerChoices.B: Whether the occupancy qualifies for the optional method being used

questionBank.explanation: The first step is determining that the optional method applies. If the wrong method is chosen, the rest of the calculation can be invalid even if the arithmetic is correct.

questionBank.questionText: In a hazardous-location problem, what should generally be established before selecting equipment or fittings?
questionBank.explanation: Classification comes first. Once the Class, Division, or Zone is known, the candidate can move to the appropriate equipment and wiring-method rules.

questionBank.questionText: Which NEC section is commonly used to explain the hierarchy between general rules and rules for special occupancies, equipment, or conditions?
questionBank.explanation: Section 90.3 explains the arrangement of the Code and the relationship between Chapters 1 through 4 and the special rules in Chapters 5 through 7.

questionBank.questionText: According to official NFPA development material for the 2026 NEC, which new article was created to address grounding and bonding of systems over 1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc nominal?
questionBank.explanation: NFPA working-draft material for the 2026 cycle shows the creation of Article 270 for grounding and bonding of systems over 1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc nominal.

questionBank.questionText: Official NFPA public-input material for the 2026 cycle proposed a new Article 627 primarily to address power transfer for what broad category?

answerChoices.B: Electric self-propelled vehicles beyond standard on-road EV coverage

questionBank.explanation: NFPA 2026 public-input records describe Article 627 as addressing electric self-propelled vehicle power transfer systems, expanding beyond only on-road electric vehicles.

practiceExams.title: Apprentice National Core Practice Exam
practiceExams.structureNotes: Starter national-core simulation focused on code navigation, safety, branch circuits, boxes, and conductors. Not a jurisdiction-specific official exam form.

practiceExams.title: Journeyman National Core Practice Exam
practiceExams.structureNotes: Starter national-core simulation emphasizing continuous loads, feeder questions, grounding, motors, and load calculations. Texas should receive a separate knowledge-versus-calculation overlay because TDLR changed the journeyman exam to two parts effective March 11, 2025.

practiceExams.title: Master National Core Practice Exam
practiceExams.structureNotes: Starter national-core simulation emphasizing calculation method choice, hazardous locations, code hierarchy, and 2026-cycle change awareness. Maine official materials show 100-question master exams with a 70 percent passing score, while ICC notes many contractor/trades exams require 70 percent and some master electrician exams require 75 percent depending on the bulletin.

flashcards.back: A load where the maximum current is expected to continue for 3 hours or more. This definition drives several sizing rules.
A defined NEC term that should be checked in Article 100 before answering access questions.
General rules for access and working space around electrical equipment are commonly found in 110.26.
Grounding connects to earth for stabilization and voltage limitation. Bonding joins conductive parts to establish an effective fault-current path.
A master-level load-calculation path that must only be used when the occupancy and problem conditions qualify.
Section 90.3 explains how Chapters 1 through 4 interact with special rules in Chapters 5 through 7.
Common starting point for general conductor ampacity lookup before applying other applicable rules.
2026-cycle NFPA development material shows Article 270 created for grounding and bonding of systems over 1000 Vac and 1500 Vdc nominal.
2026-cycle NFPA public-input material proposed Article 627 for electric self-propelled vehicle power transfer systems.

quickReferenceGuides.title: Continuous-Load Workflow
bullets: Step 1: Confirm the load is continuous under Article 100.
Step 2: Identify whether the question asks for load, conductor, overcurrent device, or all three.
Step 3: Apply the relevant 125 percent rule where required.
Step 4: Finish the code path in conductor and overcurrent articles.

quickReferenceGuides.title: Grounding and Bonding Map
bullets: 250.32: Buildings or structures supplied by feeder or branch circuit

quickReferenceGuides.title: Motor Question Workflow
bullets: Identify whether the question asks about conductors, overloads, or short-circuit and ground-fault protection.
Use the applicable motor table or section specified by the NEC.
Do not assume nameplate current is always the controlling value.

Week 12: Timed final simulations and jurisdiction overlays

glossary.definition: A load where the maximum current is expected to continue for 3 hours or more.
A defined NEC access term that should be checked in Article 100 whenever availability or access affects the answer.
Connecting to ground or to a conductive body that serves in place of ground for system stabilization and voltage limitation purposes.
Permanent joining of metallic parts to form an electrically conductive path that helps ensure electrical continuity and fault-current capability.
The grounding electrodes present at a building or structure that are bonded together as required by the NEC.
A calculation method permitted by the NEC for certain occupancies and conditions as an alternative to the standard method.
A location where fire or explosion hazards may exist because of flammable gases, vapors, liquids, dusts, or fibers.
Equipment installed for transferring energy between premises wiring and an electric vehicle.
A term undergoing notable 2026-cycle refinement in official NFPA development records; WattWise should treat wording carefully until final text is licensed and verified.
The disconnecting means for service conductors, central to service-equipment and bonding questions.

jurisdictionResearchNotes.summary: There is no single national electrician license exam. WattWise should ship a national NEC core plus jurisdiction overlays for approval rules, vendor, question counts, pass thresholds, and local amendments.
jurisdictionResearchNotes.officialSource: Use the official licensing board and vendor bulletin for each jurisdiction before publishing production exam claims.

jurisdictionResearchNotes.summary: California DLSE states applicants are approved through the Electrician Certification Unit and then schedule with PSI. The PSI Candidate Information Bulletin is part of the official process after approval.

TDLR states that, starting March 11, 2025, the Journeyman Electrician exam changed to 2 separate parts: a knowledge portion and a calculations portion. Texas therefore needs a distinct journeyman exam experience in WattWise.
Virginia DPOR requires board pre-approval before the exam, uses PSI for Tradesmen exams, lists separate journeyman and master exam fees, and links candidates to a Candidate Information Bulletin PDF.

Maine uses Prov for its examination program. Official board pages state that the journeyman exam is 80 questions with 70 percent required to pass, the master exam is 100 questions with 70 percent required to pass, and 15 hours of current NEC update training are required before renewal for journeyman and master licensees.

ICC states that many contractor/trades exams are open book and that the exam catalog contains the current bulletin, outline, references, and administration details. ICC support guidance notes that most contractor/trades exams require 70 percent and that the Master Electrician exam requires 75 percent where applicable.

Washington L&I publishes electrician exam pathways based on specialty and journey level. The official exam page lists classroom-hour and experience-hour thresholds before candidates apply, making Washington a strong example of a state where apprenticeship records and exam eligibility are tightly linked.

Oregon BCD treats electrical individual and contractor licensing as part of a broader licensing system with exam locations, reciprocal-license guidance, continuing education, and distinct individual-license pathways. Oregon therefore needs both exam prep and licensing-process support content.

Florida DBPR examination information states approved candidates schedule computer-based construction exams through Pearson VUE. Florida is a reminder that some states emphasize contractor examination and business/regulatory pathways rather than a statewide journeyman license model.

sourceList.title: ICC Jurisdiction-Specific Contractor/Trades Exams

sourceList.title: Are the exams open-book?
sourceList.notes: States that most ICC exams are open book and candidates must use the exam catalog bulletin.

sourceList.title: What score do I need to pass?
sourceList.notes: States most contractor/trades exams require 70 percent and master electrician requires 75 percent where applicable.
